Blender · PyTorch · Faster R-CNN · Segment Anything

The Chess Simulator

A procedural 3D chess set, rendered straight-on so every piece is seen in profile — and two computer-vision systems that read a position back from an image: one for the renderer's own boards, and one for real photographs, driving a live webcam recognizer.

12positions · 8 board styles
99.5%real photos · held-out test
100%synthetic · held-out test
livewebcam recognition

I · The Set

Board and Staunton-style pieces are modelled procedurally and rendered from a straight-on, slightly-raised camera — square to the board with no diagonal skew, so each piece stands in profile while the whole board and all eight ranks stay in view. Eight board styles: three woods, two marbles, three flat colours.

II · The Replica

A photoreal recreation of the owner's actual set: the cream-and-black vinyl roll-up coordinate board and silicone Club Classic tournament pieces. Piece geometry was matched against the owner's own photos — ribbed-mane knight, coronet queen with nested ball, flat cross-pattée king — and every piece is scaled to proportions measured from a straight-on photo of the real pieces. Rendered in Cycles under HDRI room light with a phone-camera finishing pass; the lineups restage the owner's own comparison shots.

III · Reading Real Photographs

A Faster R-CNN, pretrained on COCO and fine-tuned on real photos of a tournament board with heavy lighting/blur/noise augmentation, finds and names every piece directly from a photograph. Green outlines are white pieces; orange are black.

Honest scope. Trained and tested on disjoint splits of one public dataset — a single vinyl board and plastic set. The 99.5% precision / 99.5% recall is on the 58 held-out test photos the model never saw in training, so it is a true measure for boards like that one. On a very different set, lighting, or angle it will do worse; broad robustness would need photos of many more sets. This is the legitimate first step, not a solved problem.

IV · The Analyzer — Two Tabs

The real-photo detector runs behind a small local web app with two tabs. It runs on your own machine because the page needs to reach the model directly — a hosted page can't.

↑Upload a photo

Drop or choose any chess photo; the detector runs once and labels every piece it finds, with a running tally by colour and type.

●Live camera

Point your webcam at a board and it labels the pieces every frame, following them as you move the board or camera — ~5–8 fps on the GPU.

# from the project folder — starts the analyzer (both tabs)
D:\chess-vision\venv\Scripts\python.exe vision\live_server.py
# then open http://localhost:8173 in Chrome

V · The Synthetic Engine

Because the renderer knows exactly where every piece is, it labels its own training data — pixel-exact boxes and a per-piece visibility mask for every scene, no hand annotation. A Mask R-CNN trained on those masks reads the renderer's own boards.

VI · Scorecard

Every number below is measured on data the model was not trained on — separate held-out test sets in both cases.

Measure separate, then learn from everything. The scores are measured with the test set held strictly out of training, so they're honest estimates of performance on unseen boards. The model you actually use in the analyzer is then retrained on all the labelled data — train, validation and test combined — so nothing is wasted. Keeping them apart is for the measurement; the deployed model still learns from every example.
RecognizerTested onPrecisionRecall
Real photographs
Faster R-CNN		 58 held-out real photos99.5%99.5%
Synthetic boards
Mask R-CNN		 40 held-out synthetic scenes99.0%97.7%
Combined (owner's set)
Faster R-CNN, photo-traced pieces		 58 real + 48 digital held-out99.5% / 96.5%99.5% / 98.6%

VII · Test it yourself

Upload a photo or a video of a board, or point your webcam at one, and the recognizer marks every piece live. Inference runs on the local engine (vision/live_server.py on this computer) — this page just talks to it, so it works only in a browser on the machine where the engine is running.

Checking whether the local engine is running…

Renders and self-labelled training data from make_chess_scenes.py and vision/generate_dataset.py; the real detector from vision/train_real_detector.py (Roboflow chess dataset), the synthetic one from vision/train_detector.py; the live app is vision/live_server.py. Training and test data are kept in disjoint splits throughout, so no accuracy figure is reported on data the model has seen.
